title: Lesson 201
transcript: We spent a bunch of time talking about all the different types of controls that we have and all the different categories of controls and functional and assurance aspects of controls. So how do we go about deciding how much control, how many controls to put in place, how much security to put in place? And fundamentally, this comes down to a decision, a very simple decision, and it's basically how much security is cost justified. It's always a balance between the cost of protecting an asset against the level of acceptable risk, which fundamentally is about every control needs to be cost justified. It has to help the organization achieve its goals and objectives. And importantly, the control can't cost more than the actual risk that you're trying to cover. So what are some of the decisions, the determining factors that we use in actually selecting a particular control that we're gonna use? Here are some of the major pieces that we might look at. We might look at exactly how well does this control align to the business goals and objectives? How does this control actually help the business achieve its goals and objectives? Is this control cost effective? Does this control provide both the functional and assurance aspects that every control should provide? The final piece that we want to look at for every control is, is it a complete control? Does it provide a combination of both preventive, detective, and corrective aspects? So these are the major pieces that we would look at when determining what controls we want in place. Once we have controls actually put in place, we want to make sure those controls are actually working correctly. And that's where we start to talk about metrics and measuring and making sure that these controls are working correctly. And when it comes to defining risk metrics, There's a lot of different things that we could measure. And so what it really boils down to with measurement is actually figuring out what metrics we can measure, but more importantly, who's our audience? Who are we actually presenting these metrics to? And what do they actually need to know from these metrics to make good business decisions? That's where I love the term metrics that matter. You want to pick metrics that actually make sense to the audience. So that's a big part of measuring effectiveness of controls is picking the right metrics and reporting them in the right way to the right people. Another really important aspect of risk management is this concept of continuous improvement. And by that, I mean it's a very long, arduous process to go through the entire risk analysis process, the risk management process, to think about our most valuable assets, to identify all the risks associated with those assets, and then figure out all the treatment. It can sometimes takes months, even years to go through that entire process. And when an organization's done, it's not like they get to just kind of be like, done finally with that. I'm out. Never doing that again. No, I mean, it's a continuous process. You have to constantly refresh this risk management process. Why? Well, it's because the environment is constantly changing. There's constantly new threats out there. There's new vulnerabilities appearing within the environment. The number of the assets that we have that we need to protect are constantly being added and removed. So the whole risk management landscape is constantly changing. And so we have to have a model of constantly refreshing our risk management process. What's the perfect timing for how often you should redo your risk management, redo the process? There is no one precise timing, date, anything like that. The perfect answer is as often as necessary. What does that mean? Well, whenever there is a significant new threat, a new vulnerability, a new asset in the environment, that should trigger the risk management process for those pieces. And so when we talk about continuously updating, this is what it really means. We're triggering the update of our risk management process by significant changes in our environment. Thank you.
